A Proposal

Emily & Lucas

Shinto ceremony at Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine

When
Early–Mid October 2026
Where
Fujiyoshida, Yamanashi
Guests
~10
The Venue

Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine

The shrine sits at the northern base of Mount Fuji, inside a dense old-growth cedar forest. Established in 788, dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime (the Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji), and part of the Fujisan UNESCO World Heritage listing. It holds weddings.

The approach is a long stone-lantern path under thousand-year-old cedars, through an eighteen-meter-wide red torii gate. The ceremony takes place in the haiden. October in Fujiyoshida is comfortable during the day (18 to 19 degrees) but cools quickly after 3pm. Sunset is at 5:10pm.

We recommend the 11am ceremony slot. Good natural light for photos afterwards and a relaxed transition into the meal.

Juri is confirming October availability and the shrine's ceremony fee directly.

Schedule

The Day

8:00 AM

Dressing

Emily's shiromuku preparation begins in a hotel suite near the shrine. About two hours for the bride: kimono fitted layer by layer, traditional wig set and secured, hair and makeup in parallel. Lucas's montsuki haori hakama takes about thirty minutes. Juri is with you both from the start.

10:30 AM

Arrival

We transport the full group to the shrine. One of us meets your grandmother and mother at the entrance and walks them through. Lucas and other guests arrive separately and are seated.

11:00 AM

Ceremony

About thirty minutes. The shrine priest leads the purification rite, reads the norito prayer, and conducts the san-san-kudo sake exchange. Ring exchange can be included. Your family is seated across from you.

11:45 AM

Photos

Portraits on the shrine grounds: the cedar approach, the torii, the main hall. You and Lucas, then family groupings, then the three generations together. October light in the grove is soft and diffused. We coordinate every setup and keep the pace comfortable for your grandmother.

1:00 PM

Lunch

Private kaiseki lunch. Ten guests, one private room, ground-floor access. Seasonal courses, no time pressure. Details below.

Post-Ceremony

The Meal

Two options we are exploring:

Recommended

Hotel Kaneyamaen

Traditional ryokan in Fujiyoshida, ten minutes from the shrine. Private kaiseki dining rooms, 25,000 sqm of Japanese gardens. Ground-floor access available. The ryokan also has guest rooms if you want to stay the night.

Alternative

Sousaku Kappo Gin

Kappo restaurant in Fujiyoshida. Chef trained at a two-star kappo in Kanazawa. Course-only, private rooms, seats up to 40. More intimate, more local. Weekend lunch available by reservation.

We confirm the venue once Juri has spoken with both directly. The priority: private room, ground-floor access, appropriate for your grandmother, no time pressure.

Traditional Dress

Attire

The Bride

Shiromuku

Traditional all-white bridal kimono for Shinto ceremonies. Three layers, white silk. We source from established rental houses in Tokyo and ship to the shrine. A professional dresser handles everything on the morning of.

The Wig

Bunkin-Takashimada

We recommend the traditional wig over styling your own hair. It photographs better in the shrine setting, and the makers we work with produce lightweight pieces. Your hair type makes the wig a better foundation for the silhouette. Requires a fitting trip to Tokyo in late July or August.

The Groom

Montsuki Haori Hakama

Black silk kimono with family crests, haori jacket, hakama trousers. The right choice next to a shiromuku at a shrine. No in-person fitting needed. We confirm Lucas's measurements eight weeks out and handle rental and delivery.

If your mother or grandmother would like to wear kimono on the day, we coordinate additional rentals and dressers. Common request, easy to add.

Photography

Photographer

We source a photographer who has shot Shinto shrine ceremonies, works well in muted natural light, and is comfortable with small family gatherings. Coverage from start of dressing through end of meal. Edited gallery and print release included.

We will present a specific recommendation with portfolio samples once Juri has sourced options in the Fujiyoshida area.

Scope

What We Handle

Before the Trip

  • Shrine booking, ceremony time slot, fee coordination
  • Kimono selection, rental, fitting logistics for Emily
  • Wig fitting coordination and scheduling
  • Montsuki rental, sizing, delivery for Lucas
  • Photographer sourcing and booking
  • Meal venue booking and private room coordination
  • Hair and makeup artist booking
  • Dresser team coordination
  • Written day-of timeline shared with you, your mother, and your grandmother
  • All Japanese-language communication with every vendor and the shrine office

On the Ground

  • One or both of us physically present from dressing to end of meal
  • Bilingual support for Lucas and English-speaking guests
  • Ground transport for the full group
  • Vendor and dresser management on the morning of
  • Photographer direction and family grouping coordination
  • Grandmother and family support: timing, seating, rest breaks
  • Point of contact for every logistical question so your family does not have to be

What You Handle

  • International flights for you and Lucas
  • Accommodation in the Fujiyoshida/Kawaguchiko area
  • Communication with your family about the trip
  • Lucas's measurements to us by August
  • Emily's fitting trip to Tokyo (late July or August, 3 to 4 days)
Every Japanese-language conversation about your wedding runs through Juri. Your mother and grandmother do not need to make a single phone call.
Investment

Cost

Planning Fee
$5,000 USD
All planning, coordination, vendor management, and day-of bilingual support

Estimated Vendor and Venue Costs

Above the planning fee. Ranges reflect quality tier and options selected.

Shrine ceremony fee$1,800 – $2,200
Bridal kimono (shiromuku, wig, dresser)$2,500 – $4,000
Groom's montsuki haori hakama$400 – $700
Hair and makeup$600 – $900
Photography (full day)$2,500 – $4,000
Private kaiseki lunch (10 guests)$1,500 – $2,500
Ground transport$400 – $700
Estimated Total (incl. planning fee)$14,700 – $20,000

These are realistic figures for a traditional Shinto ceremony at this shrine, done properly. A shorter photography block or a simpler lunch venue bring the total down. We would rather give you the honest number upfront than quote low and adjust later.

All costs quoted in USD. Vendor payments in yen converted at prevailing rate at time of payment.
